toolUseResult.content:
# Overhead model

Definitions and policy for what `tprobe` calls "overhead"
and what gets subtracted from measured values.

## Measured quantities

Calibration runs a two-point fit on an empty `black_box(1)`
bench and produces two numbers, both recorded on the
`Overhead` struct.

### Framing — `framing_ticks` (per scope)

The hardware cost of the two `rdtsc` reads that bracket
every `TProbe` scope. Paid once per scope, independent of
how many events the scope covers. On modern x86 this is
~20 ticks (~5 ns at ~3.7 GHz).

Why two-point (and not single-point)? `rdtsc` is not a
serializing instruction, so two back-to-back reads with
nothing between them can execute out-of-order and measure
as little as 0–1 ticks — well below the true apparatus
cost. Bracketing with `_mm_lfence` drains the pipeline
harder than real work does and overcorrects. The two-point
fit avoids both biases by varying an `inner` size and
solving

```
raw_delta = framing + inner · loop_per_iter
```

in `f64` arithmetic (no integer truncation) across two
empty benches at `N_LOW = 100` and `N_HIGH = 10_000`. The
intercept is framing; the slope is `loop_per_iter` (below).

Noise amplification on the intercept is
`d(framing) / d(raw_low) = N_HIGH / (N_HIGH − N_LOW) ≈ 1.01`
with the current constants — any slop on `raw_low`
propagates roughly 1:1 to framing.

### Loop-per-iter — `loop_per_iter_ticks` (per inner iter)

The slope of the same fit — the per-iteration cost of the
calibration's empty inner loop, one `black_box(1)` per
iteration. On modern x86 this is small, on the order of one
tick per iter.

It models the loop scaffolding a real measurement loop also
pays: loop branch, counter increment, plus whatever cost a
"does-nothing" iteration has. It is not a perfect model of
a real dispatch loop — the real loop doesn't execute the
`black_box(1)` itself, so its per-iter scaffolding is
slightly cheaper than what calibration reports.

## Unmeasurable overhead

Things calibration does not separately measure and that are
not separately subtractable from stored scope deltas:

- Cache misses and branch mispredicts inside the scope.
- Context switches and OS interrupts during the scope.
- Microarchitectural noise (TLB misses, frequency changes,
  thermal throttling).

These are part of what the real system experiences and
remain in the measurement. The bot thinks they typically
manifest in the `p99-max` tail of the histogram — which is
why the band-table report also prints `mean min-p99`
(whole-distribution statistics excluding the top 1 % tail,
robust against rare outliers).

No future calibration can separate these per-event without
a fundamentally different measurement technique; they are
the irreducible noise floor of "whatever happened between
the two `rdtsc` reads". Keeping them in the measurement is
honest — they are part of the real cost the measured code
pays.

## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value. Currently:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch
                └── amortized over the scope
```

At `batch = 1` the full framing cost is paid per event; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events, rounding toward
zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is kept on the
`Overhead` struct for diagnostic visibility but is not
currently subtracted.

The bot thinks extending subtraction to include
`loop_per_iter_ticks` is the right call: loop scaffolding
is overhead relative to the dispatch work, and the
`black_box(1)` iter cost is a close-enough proxy. The
tradeoff is a small downward bias on reported values
because the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)`
itself; the bias is on the order of a tick per event and
worth the simpler story in the output. That change is
planned for a later step in the 0.3.0 ladder; this document
gets revised then.

## Per-event correction when batches mix

Today every record in a given probe drain carries the same
`batch` (the runtime commits to one `inner` per run), so
`correction` is a single constant across the drain. If a
future probe ever mixes `batch` values within one drain —
e.g. an actor whose scopes cover both fast-path singles
(`batch = 1`, framing term paid in full) and slow-path
batches (`batch = 1000`, framing term ≈ 0) — the correct
overhead-adjusted mean per band requires per-record
correction tracked per band, not a single global average.
The bot thinks the change at that point is:

- drain loop accumulates `band_correction_sum[i]` alongside
  `band_sum[i]`,
- `adj_mean[i] = (band_sum[i] - band_correction_sum[i]) /
  band_count[i]`.

Deferred until that situation arises.

input.content:
# tprobe

A named, single-writer performance probe that records
hardware tick-counter deltas across scoped code regions and
renders a percentile band-table report.

Part of the [actor-x1 workspace](../..). Originally vendored
from [`iiac-perf`](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf);
extracted into its own crate at `actor-x1 0.2.0`.

## At a glance

```rust
use tprobe::{TProbe, calibrate};

let mut probe = TProbe::new("dispatch");
let ovh = calibrate();

for _ in 0..n {
    let id = probe.start(site_id);
    // ... work to measure ...
    probe.end(id);
}

probe.report(/* as_ticks = */ false, Some(&ovh));
```

## Public surface

- `TProbe` / `TProbeRecId` — scope-based probe
  (`start` / `end` / `end_batch` → record buffer →
  histogram on `report`).
- `Overhead` / `calibrate` — apparatus-overhead calibration
  via a two-point fit.
- Modules `pin` (CPU affinity) and `ticks` (hardware tick
  counter).

## Notes

- [`notes/design.md`](notes/design.md) — architecture and
  hot-path / cold-path split.
- [`notes/overhead-model.md`](notes/overhead-model.md) —
  formal overhead model: what framing is, what
  `loop_per_iter` is, which overhead is unmeasurable and
  stays in the measurement, and the current subtraction
  policy.

## Platform

x86_64 only; `ticks.rs` emits `compile_error!` on other
architectures.

## License

Dual-licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0.

input.content:
# tprobe design

A named, single-writer histogram of hardware tick-counter
deltas, plus a record buffer, plus a percentile band-table
renderer. Tuned for the hot path to be as short as possible
— two `rdtsc` reads and a `Vec::push` — with delta math,
histogram ingestion, and tick→ns conversion deferred to
report time.

## Data flow

```
start(site_id)  ─┐
                 │
end(id)          ├─► Record { site_id, start_tsc, end_tsc, batch }
                 │             └─ appended to records: Vec<Record>
end_batch(id, n) ┘

                            report(as_ticks, overhead)
                                   │
                                   ▼
                        records.drain(..) →
                          per_event = (delta - correction) / batch
                                   │
                                   ▼
                             Histogram<u64>
                                   │
                                   ▼
                          band_table::render
```

- **Hot path** is `start` + `end*`: two `rdtsc` reads and a
  `Vec::push`. No division, no histogram updates, no unit
  conversion.
- **Cold path** is `report`: drains the record buffer,
  applies per-record division and overhead correction,
  ingests into the histogram, then renders the band table.
- **`clear`** zeroes both the record buffer and the
  histogram. Used at the warmup → measurement boundary so
  warmup samples don't contaminate the reported
  distribution.

## Modules

- `tprobe` — the probe type (`TProbe`, `TProbeRecId`) and
  the `start` / `end` / `end_batch` / `clear` / `report`
  surface.
- `overhead` — calibration and the `Overhead` struct. See
  [`overhead-model.md`](overhead-model.md) for the formal
  model.
- `band_table` — percentile-band renderer (12 bands,
  `first` / `last` / `range` / `count` / `mean` columns,
  summary rows). Shared by the `TProbe` report path.
- `ticks` — hardware tick-counter abstraction. x86_64 only;
  uses `rdtsc` via the `minstant` crate. Other architectures
  emit `compile_error!`.
- `fmt` — `fmt_commas` / `fmt_commas_f64`, extracted from
  `iiac-perf`'s harness so we don't carry the whole 393-line
  benchmark harness for two formatting helpers.
- `pin` — CPU-affinity helpers (`parse_cores`,
  `pin_current`). Adapted from `iiac-perf`'s `pin.rs`.

## Upstream

Vendored from
[`iiac-perf`](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf) —
source path at the time of vendoring: `../iiac-perf/src`.
Graduated from `actor-x1`'s in-tree `src/perf/` module at
`actor-x1 0.2.0-2`.

Divergences from upstream at the time of graduation:

- Import paths reflect the standalone-crate layout
  (`crate::x` rather than `crate::perf::x`).
- `// OK: …` justifications on every `unwrap*` call per the
  host workspace's code conventions.
- `ticks.rs` diagnostic string mentions "tprobe" rather than
  "iiac-perf".
- `TProbe` here corresponds to upstream's scope-API probe
  (`TProbe2` in `iiac-perf`); the upstream fast-path
  direct-histogram `TProbe` was not vendored. The "2" suffix
  was dropped on extraction at `0.2.0-3`.

toolUseResult.content:
# tprobe design

A named, single-writer histogram of hardware tick-counter
deltas, plus a record buffer, plus a percentile band-table
renderer. Tuned for the hot path to be as short as possible
— two `rdtsc` reads and a `Vec::push` — with delta math,
histogram ingestion, and tick→ns conversion deferred to
report time.

## Data flow

```
start(site_id)  ─┐
                 │
end(id)          ├─► Record { site_id, start_tsc, end_tsc, batch }
                 │             └─ appended to records: Vec<Record>
end_batch(id, n) ┘

                            report(as_ticks, overhead)
                                   │
                                   ▼
                        records.drain(..) →
                          per_event = (delta - correction) / batch
                                   │
                                   ▼
                             Histogram<u64>
                                   │
                                   ▼
                          band_table::render
```

- **Hot path** is `start` + `end*`: two `rdtsc` reads and a
  `Vec::push`. No division, no histogram updates, no unit
  conversion.
- **Cold path** is `report`: drains the record buffer,
  applies per-record division and overhead correction,
  ingests into the histogram, then renders the band table.
- **`clear`** zeroes both the record buffer and the
  histogram. Used at the warmup → measurement boundary so
  warmup samples don't contaminate the reported
  distribution.

## Modules

- `tprobe` — the probe type (`TProbe`, `TProbeRecId`) and
  the `start` / `end` / `end_batch` / `clear` / `report`
  surface.
- `overhead` — calibration and the `Overhead` struct. See
  [`overhead-model.md`](overhead-model.md) for the formal
  model.
- `band_table` — percentile-band renderer (12 bands,
  `first` / `last` / `range` / `count` / `mean` columns,
  summary rows). Shared by the `TProbe` report path.
- `ticks` — hardware tick-counter abstraction. x86_64 only;
  uses `rdtsc` via the `minstant` crate. Other architectures
  emit `compile_error!`.
- `fmt` — `fmt_commas` / `fmt_commas_f64`, extracted from
  `iiac-perf`'s harness so we don't carry the whole 393-line
  benchmark harness for two formatting helpers.
- `pin` — CPU-affinity helpers (`parse_cores`,
  `pin_current`). Adapted from `iiac-perf`'s `pin.rs`.

## Upstream

Vendored from
[`iiac-perf`](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf) —
source path at the time of vendoring: `../iiac-perf/src`.
Graduated from `actor-x1`'s in-tree `src/perf/` module at
`actor-x1 0.2.0-2`.

Divergences from upstream at the time of graduation:

- Import paths reflect the standalone-crate layout
  (`crate::x` rather than `crate::perf::x`).
- `// OK: …` justifications on every `unwrap*` call per the
  host workspace's code conventions.
- `ticks.rs` diagnostic string mentions "tprobe" rather than
  "iiac-perf".
- `TProbe` here corresponds to upstream's scope-API probe
  (`TProbe2` in `iiac-perf`); the upstream fast-path
  direct-histogram `TProbe` was not vendored. The "2" suffix
  was dropped on extraction at `0.2.0-3`.

input.content:
# Overhead model

Definitions and policy for what `tprobe` calls "overhead"
and what gets subtracted from measured values.

## Measured quantities

Calibration runs a two-point fit on an empty `black_box(1)`
bench and produces two numbers, both recorded on the
`Overhead` struct.

### Framing — `framing_ticks` (per scope)

The hardware cost of the two `rdtsc` reads that bracket
every `TProbe` scope. Paid once per scope, independent of
how many events the scope covers. On modern x86 this is
~20 ticks (~5 ns at ~3.7 GHz).

Why two-point (and not single-point)? `rdtsc` is not a
serializing instruction, so two back-to-back reads with
nothing between them can execute out-of-order and measure
as little as 0–1 ticks — well below the true apparatus
cost. Bracketing with `_mm_lfence` drains the pipeline
harder than real work does and overcorrects. The two-point
fit avoids both biases by varying an `inner` size and
solving

```
raw_delta = framing + inner · loop_per_iter
```

in `f64` arithmetic (no integer truncation) across two
empty benches at `N_LOW = 100` and `N_HIGH = 10_000`. The
intercept is framing; the slope is `loop_per_iter` (below).

Noise amplification on the intercept is
`d(framing) / d(raw_low) = N_HIGH / (N_HIGH − N_LOW) ≈ 1.01`
with the current constants — any slop on `raw_low`
propagates roughly 1:1 to framing.

### Loop-per-iter — `loop_per_iter_ticks` (per inner iter)

The slope of the same fit — the per-iteration cost of the
calibration's empty inner loop, one `black_box(1)` per
iteration. On modern x86 this is small, on the order of one
tick per iter.

It models the loop scaffolding a real measurement loop also
pays: loop branch, counter increment, plus whatever cost a
"does-nothing" iteration has. It is not a perfect model of
a real dispatch loop — the real loop doesn't execute the
`black_box(1)` itself, so its per-iter scaffolding is
slightly cheaper than what calibration reports.

## Unmeasurable overhead

Things calibration does not separately measure and that are
not separately subtractable from stored scope deltas:

- Cache misses and branch mispredicts inside the scope.
- Context switches and OS interrupts during the scope.
- Microarchitectural noise (TLB misses, frequency changes,
  thermal throttling).

These are part of what the real system experiences and
remain in the measurement. The bot thinks they typically
manifest in the `p99-max` tail of the histogram — which is
why the band-table report also prints `mean min-p99`
(whole-distribution statistics excluding the top 1 % tail,
robust against rare outliers).

No future calibration can separate these per-event without
a fundamentally different measurement technique; they are
the irreducible noise floor of "whatever happened between
the two `rdtsc` reads". Keeping them in the measurement is
honest — they are part of the real cost the measured code
pays.

## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value. Currently:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch
                └── amortized over the scope
```

At `batch = 1` the full framing cost is paid per event; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events, rounding toward
zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is kept on the
`Overhead` struct for diagnostic visibility but is not
currently subtracted.

The bot thinks extending subtraction to include
`loop_per_iter_ticks` is the right call: loop scaffolding
is overhead relative to the dispatch work, and the
`black_box(1)` iter cost is a close-enough proxy. The
tradeoff is a small downward bias on reported values
because the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)`
itself; the bias is on the order of a tick per event and
worth the simpler story in the output. That change is
planned for a later step in the 0.3.0 ladder; this document
gets revised then.

## Per-event correction when batches mix

Today every record in a given probe drain carries the same
`batch` (the runtime commits to one `inner` per run), so
`correction` is a single constant across the drain. If a
future probe ever mixes `batch` values within one drain —
e.g. an actor whose scopes cover both fast-path singles
(`batch = 1`, framing term paid in full) and slow-path
batches (`batch = 1000`, framing term ≈ 0) — the correct
overhead-adjusted mean per band requires per-record
correction tracked per band, not a single global average.
The bot thinks the change at that point is:

- drain loop accumulates `band_correction_sum[i]` alongside
  `band_sum[i]`,
- `adj_mean[i] = (band_sum[i] - band_correction_sum[i]) /
  band_count[i]`.

Deferred until that situation arises.

content.content:
1	//! actor-x1 — an experiment in the actor model in Rust.
2	//!
3	//! See `notes/design.md` for the staged design. Stage 1 provides
4	//! a minimal runtime with two actors that ping-pong an empty
5	//! [`Message`] for a caller-supplied duration; Goal1 runs both
6	//! actors on one thread, Goal2 runs each actor on its own thread.
7	//!
8	//! Public surface today:
9	//! - [`Message`]: the empty unit message Stage 1 actors exchange.
10	//! - [`Actor`]: the single-method actor trait.
11	//! - [`Context`]: the send-side handle passed to each dispatch.
12	//! - [`runtime`]: concrete runtimes (single-thread for Goal1; the
13	//!   multi-thread runtime will land in 0.1.0-3).
14	
15	pub mod runtime;
16	
17	/// Empty unit-type message exchanged between Stage 1 actors.
18	/// Carries no payload by design — Stage 1's contract is only
19	/// "dispatch happens"; Stage 2 replaces this with a richer type.
20	pub struct Message;
21	
22	/// Actor behavior: handle one [`Message`] at a time, never blocking.
23	///
24	/// The only method is [`Actor::handle_message`]; all outbound
25	/// communication goes through the [`Context`] the runtime supplies.
26	pub trait Actor {
27	    /// Handle a single incoming message. Use `ctx` to emit any
28	    /// resulting sends. Must not block.
29	    fn handle_message(&mut self, ctx: &mut dyn Context, msg: Message);
30	}
31	
32	/// Runtime-supplied send handle passed to each
33	/// [`Actor::handle_message`] call. Implementations differ between
34	/// runtimes: the single-thread runtime writes to a shared queue;
35	/// the (future) multi-thread runtime writes to per-actor channels.
36	pub trait Context {
37	    /// Enqueue `msg` for delivery to the actor identified by
38	    /// `dst_id`. Returns immediately; delivery is the runtime's job.
39	    fn send(&mut self, dst_id: u32, msg: Message);
40	}
41

content.subject: 0.3.0-3: band-table raw + adj mean column
content.description: TProbe::report stores raw per_event in histogram (no correction applied). band_table::render gains an optional per-event correction parameter; columns first/last/range/count/mean show raw; new rightmost adj mean column shows raw − correction. Summary rows: mean and mean min-p99 get adj values in the new column; stdev rows leave it blank (shift-invariant). Use global avg correction (approach a — exact for today's constant-batch PoC).

content.subject: 0.3.0-2: extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter
content.description: Change Overhead::per_event_ticks to include both framing/batch and loop_per_iter_ticks. Update apparatus: line to show the split so readers see what was subtracted. Update tests. No flag/UX change yet.

Write crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md defining framing, loop-per-iter, and the "everything else" category (unmeasurable: cache misses, context switches, interrupts — stays in the number). Update code doc-comments in overhead.rs / tprobe.rs to point at overhead-model.md instead of carrying the long prose inline. Docs-only; no behavior change.

# Notes

See [design.md](design.md)


## jj tips

For users new to jj see [jj-tips.md](jj-tips.md).

```
## Chores format

Filename: "chores-XX.md"
example: chores-01.md

Format of section labels: "## <short description> (X.Y.Z)"
example: "## Topic format description (0.1.0)"

Example chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Do something (1.3.1)

Describe something
```

## Versioning during development

This is using jujustiu, jj + git and we'll see how it goes. Below is my
git workflow, jj will be different but we'll have to discover that as
we go.

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump directly to
  `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-devN`, implement
  across multiple commits, final commit removes `-devN`.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before starting.

For multi-step:
1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-devN` with a plan and commit as a chore marker
2. Implement in one or more `-devN` commits (bump N as needed)
3. Final commit removes `-devN`, updates todo/chores — this is the "done" marker

The final release commit (without `-devN`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits. This keeps the git history readable and makes it easy to see which
commits were exploratory vs final.

## Todo format

Todo.md contains two main sections "Todo" and "Done" each item is a
short explanations of a tasks and links to more details using 1 or more
references.

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
In markdown, `[2,3]` is a single ref key (won't resolve) and `[2][3]`
is parsed as display text `2` with ref key `3` (so `[2]` won't resolve).

Examples:

# Todo

- Add new feature X [details](chores-01.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [1]

# Done

- Fixed issue Z [2],[3]

[1]: chores-01bugs.md#bug-y

- `notes/README.md`: `design.md` link target updated to

- `notes/chores-01.md`: this section; historical `[design.md](design.md)`

- `notes/todo.md`: reference `[1]` retargeted to the new
  `design.md` location; plan-marker `[8]` moves from
  `## In Progress` to `## Done`; new entry for this step
  added to `## In Progress` with reference `[10]`.

### Design decisions recorded here

- **overhead-model.md reflects *current* code, not
  planned.** Policy section states framing-only subtraction
  and marks the extension to `loop_per_iter` as "planned
  for a later step in the 0.3.0 ladder". When that step
  lands, this file's subtraction-policy section gets
  updated in place. Keeps the doc in sync with code
  step-by-step rather than describing an end state that
  temporarily disagrees with what's shipping.
- **Doc comments use crate-root-relative paths
  (`notes/overhead-model.md`)**, not workspace-relative
  (`crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md`). If `tprobe` is
  ever published to crates.io, the `notes/` directory ships
  inside the crate package, so a crate-root-relative path
  resolves both in-workspace and for downstream consumers.
- **Historical chore sections retain their original text.**
  Earlier sections (Stage 1, 0.2.0 ladder) describe events
  at the time they happened; their wording is preserved,
  only the `[design.md](…)` link *targets* are updated so
  the references continue to resolve after the move.
- **Vendoring history lives in
  `crates/tprobe/notes/design.md`.** Previously the
  `tprobe` crate's `lib.rs` carried the full upstream
  divergence list. Moving it to the notes file makes
  `lib.rs` easier to scan without losing the history; it
  remains discoverable via the pointer at the top of
  `lib.rs`.

## Notes reorg + per-crate READMEs + overhead-model.md (0.3.0-1)

Docs-only step. Creates per-crate `notes/` directories and
`README.md` files so each crate carries its own documentation
with it if/when the workspace splits into separate repos.
Moves the actor-model design from workspace `/notes/` into
`crates/actor-x1/notes/`. Writes a new
`crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md` that formalizes the
overhead vocabulary: framing, `loop_per_iter`, and the
unmeasurable "everything else" category that stays in the
measurement. Replaces long prose blocks in `overhead.rs` /
`lib.rs` with brief summaries plus pointers to the notes.
No behavior change; code edits are purely doc-comment
shortening.

- `crates/actor-x1/Cargo.toml`: `0.3.0-0` → `0.3.0-1`.

  — binaries, install command, link to `notes/design.md`,
  license pointer.
- `crates/actor-x1/src/lib.rs`: `notes/design.md` path in
  the module docstring → `crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md`.

  at-a-glance usage, public surface, links to
  `notes/design.md` and `notes/overhead-model.md`, platform
  note.
- `crates/tprobe/notes/design.md`: new. Architecture
  (records → drain → histogram → band-table), hot-path vs
  cold-path split, module layout, and upstream vendoring
  history (moved here from `lib.rs`).
- `crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md`: new. Formal
  definitions of framing and `loop_per_iter` (with the
  two-point-fit derivation), the unmeasurable overhead
  category (cache misses, context switches, interrupts,
  etc.) and why it stays in the measurement, the current

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

- Band-table format + overhead docs — plan marker (0.3.0-0) [[8]]

## Todo

See [Foramt details](README.md#todo-format)

- Initial design [[1]]
- PoC implementation of a manager
- Explore `linkme` / `inventory` for a benchmark harness on top of
  `tprobe` — self-registering benchmarks (no `main()` edits per
  benchmark); possibly reusable for actor registration too [[9]]

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.subject: 0.3.0: close ladder + README update
content.description: Final release commit (no -N suffix). Update top-level README.md to reflect: new output format with adj mean column, no --raw flag, 0.5s warmup default, new per-crate READMEs and notes layout. Move completed 0.3.0-* todos to Done.

content.subject: 0.3.0-3: band-table raw + adj mean column
content.description: TProbe::report stores raw per_event in histogram (no correction applied). band_table::render gains an optional per-event correction parameter; columns first/last/range/count/mean show raw; new rightmost adj mean column shows raw − correction. Summary rows: mean and mean min-p99 get adj values in the new column; stdev rows leave it blank (shift-invariant). Use global avg correction (approach a — exact for today's constant-batch PoC).
content.activeForm: Adding adj mean column to band table

content.subject: 0.3.0-2: extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter
content.description: Change Overhead::per_event_ticks to include both framing/batch and loop_per_iter_ticks. Update apparatus: line to show the split so readers see what was subtracted. Update tests. No flag/UX change yet.

content.subject: 0.3.0-1: notes reorg + per-crate README + overhead-model.md
content.description: Move notes/design.md → crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md. Create crates/actor-x1/notes/ and crates/tprobe/notes/. Add crates/actor-x1/README.md and crates/tprobe/README.md. Write crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md defining framing, loop-per-iter, and the "everything else" category (unmeasurable: cache misses, context switches, interrupts — stays in the number). Update code doc-comments in overhead.rs / tprobe.rs to point at overhead-model.md instead of carrying the long prose inline. Docs-only; no behavior change.

content.subject: 0.3.0-0: plan marker + version bump
content.description: Bump workspace version to 0.3.0-0. Add chores-01.md entry for the ladder. Update todo.md In Progress section.

content.subject: 0.3.0-4: remove --raw flag, always calibrate
content.description: Drop --raw CLI flag from goal1 and goal2. Calibration runs unconditionally (it's ~10ms). Remove the "raw (no overhead subtraction)" branch of the apparatus line. Update README.

content.subject: 0.3.0-5: lower warmup default to 0.5s
content.description: Change --warmup default from 10.0 to 0.5 in both goal1 and goal2. Update README.

**Summary of changes (docs-only, no behavior change):**

Moves:

New files:
- `crates/actor-x1/README.md` — brief per-crate README

attachment.snippet:
1	# Chores-01
2	
3	Discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible markdown.
4	There is also a [todo.md](todo.md) file and it tracks tasks and in
5	general there should be a chore section for each task with the why
6	and how this task will be completed.
7	
8	See [Chores format](README.md#chores-format)
9	
10	## Stage 1 runtime — plan marker (0.1.0-0)
11	
12	Begins the Stage 1 implementation described in [design.md](../crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md).
13	Multi-step ladder (see [Versioning during development](../CLAUDE.md#versioning)):
14	
15	- `0.1.0-0` — bootstrap Cargo crate + this plan marker (no behavior).
16	- `0.1.0-1` — Goal1: two actors, single thread, ping-pong an empty
17	  `Message` for a caller-supplied duration in seconds. Reports
18	  total messages handled.
19	- `0.1.0-2` — vendor `tprobe2` (and its transitive deps: `band_table`,
20	  `ticks`, `fmt_commas*`) from `../iiac-perf/src` into `src/perf/`.
21	  Wrap per-dispatch `handle_message` calls with `probe.start`/`probe.end`
22	  using `site_id = actor_id`; print a band-table report at shutdown
23	  (ns by default, `-t/--ticks` for raw ticks). The bot thinks promoting
24	  this to a shared crate is worthwhile once Stage 2 is underway, but
25	  copying in now avoids a cross-repo dependency for the PoC.
26	- `0.1.0-3` — Goal2: same workload as Goal1 but each actor on its own
27	  thread, communicating via `std::sync::mpsc` channels. Shutdown by
28	  dropping senders. Probe instrumented the same way (per-thread
29	  `TProbe2`, report on join).
30	- `0.1.0` — final: drop the `-N` suffix, update `notes/todo.md` and
31	  `notes/README.md`.
32	
33	### Design decisions recorded here
34	
35	- **Actor trait signature** deviates from the design sketch by adding
36	  `&mut self` and a `&mut dyn Context` parameter, where `Context::send(dst_id, msg)`
37	  is the only way for a handler to emit messages. Required because
38	  the empty `Message {}` carries no reply-to information and the
39	  sketched signature `fn handle_message(msg: Message)` has nowhere
40	  to thread outbound sends through.
41	- **Two `Context` implementations**: the single-threaded runtime's
42	  context writes to a `VecDeque`; the multi-threaded runtime's
43	  context holds `Sender<Message>` per destination. Actors see
44	  only `&mut dyn Context` and are agnostic to which runtime is
45	  driving them.
46	- **x86_64-only for now**: `tprobe2`'s tick counter uses `rdtsc`
47	  and `iiac-perf/src/ticks.rs` emits a `compile_error!` on other
48	  arches. Development target is x86_64 so this is acceptable; the
49	  restriction is documented at the vendored module.
50	
51	## Goal1: single-thread ping-pong runtime (0.1.0-1)
52	
53	Implements Goal1 of Stage 1: two actors, one thread, ping-pong an
54	empty [`Message`] for a caller-supplied duration in seconds, then
55	report the message count and throughput.
56	
57	- `src/lib.rs`: `Message` unit struct, `Actor` trait
58	  (`handle_message(&mut self, &mut dyn Context, Message)`),
59	  `Context` trait (`send(dst_id: u32, msg: Message)`), and
60	  `pub mod runtime`.
61	- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime` owns a
62	  `Vec<Box<dyn Actor>>` and a `VecDeque<(u32, Message)>`; `run_for`
63	  pops a message, field-split-borrows `actors` and `queue`,
64	  constructs a private `SingleCtx` wrapping the queue, dispatches,
65	  counts. Terminates on deadline or queue drain. Unit tests cover
66	  seed drain, bounded ping-pong count (11 = seed + 5·2), and
67	  sequential id assignment.
68	- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: CLI takes one positional `<duration_secs>`
69	  f64; constructs two `PingPongActor`s (each replies once per
70	  received message to its peer), seeds one message into actor 0,
71	  runs, prints `goal1: <count> messages in <secs>s (<M msg/s>)`.
72	
73	Smoke run on this box: 0.5 s → ~19.3 M messages → ~38.7 M msg/s.
74	
75	### Deviation from the design sketch
76	
77	`struct Message {}` in `design.md` is realised as a unit struct
78	`pub struct Message;` — equivalent externally, instantiates as
79	`Message` rather than `Message {}`. Matches clippy's preference for
80	empty types; trivial to convert back if literal match matters.
81	
82	## Vendored perf probe + Goal1 instrumentation (0.1.0-2)
83	
84	Copies `tprobe2` and its transitive dependencies into the crate and
85	wraps every `handle_message` dispatch with a probe scope so Goal1
86	reports per-dispatch latency alongside message count.
87	
88	- `Cargo.toml`: version bump to 0.1.0-2; add `hdrhistogram = "7"` and
89	  `minstant = "0.1"` dependencies.
90	- `src/perf/mod.rs`: new module root; documents vendor source path
91	  (`../iiac-perf/src`) and divergences (import paths, `// OK: …`
92	  on `unwrap*`, one diagnostic string).
93	- `src/perf/tprobe2.rs`: vendored from `iiac-perf/src/tprobe2.rs`.
94	  Imports rewritten to `crate::perf::{band_table, ticks}`; two
95	  `unwrap` calls gained `// OK: …` justifications.
96	- `src/perf/band_table.rs`: vendored from `iiac-perf/src/band_table.rs`.
97	  Imports rewritten to `crate::perf::fmt` and `crate::perf::ticks`;
98	  `unwrap_or(0)` calls gained `// OK: …` justifications.
99	- `src/perf/ticks.rs`: vendored unchanged except for the
100	  `compile_error!` diagnostic now mentions `actor-x1 probes`.
101	- `src/perf/ticks/x86_64.rs`: vendored unchanged.
102	- `src/perf/fmt.rs`: extracted `fmt_commas` and `fmt_commas_f64`
103	  from `iiac-perf/src/harness.rs` (lines 160–188) — avoids vendoring
104	  the 393-line harness for two helpers. `unwrap_or(0)` gained a
105	  `// OK: …` justification.
106	- `src/lib.rs`: adds `pub mod perf;`.
107	- `src/runtime.rs`: `SingleThreadRuntime::new(probe_name)` now takes
108	  a name, embeds a `TProbe2`, and wraps each `handle_message` call
109	  with `probe.start(dst_id as u64)` / `probe.end(id)`. `Default` impl
110	  dropped (new takes an arg). `probe_mut()` exposes the probe for
111	  end-of-run reporting. Unit tests pass a probe name.
112	- `src/bin/goal1.rs`: new arg parser supports `-t` / `--ticks`
113	  (raw-ticks display); runtime constructed as `"goal1.dispatch"`;
114	  calls `rt.probe_mut().report(as_ticks)` after the dispatch loop.
115	  Usage errors now `exit(2)` with a usage line instead of panicking.
116	
117	### Smoke measurements (release build)
118	
119	- `goal1 0.5` — 10.56 M messages in 0.5s (~21.1 M msg/s).
120	  Per-dispatch: mean 9 ns, mean min-p99 9 ns, p99 max 67.5 µs
121	  (likely an OS interrupt in the tail).
122	- `goal1 0.5 -t` — 10.57 M messages in 0.5s (~21.1 M msg/s).
123	  Per-dispatch: mean 35 tk, mean min-p99 34 tk, p99 max 114,687 tk.
124	
125	Adding the probe cuts throughput roughly in half vs. 0.1.0-1
126	(~38.7 → ~21.1 M msg/s). The bot thinks the delta is dominated by
127	two `rdtsc` reads plus `Vec::push` per dispatch (~20 ns of the
128	~47 ns per-dispatch cost); the 9 ns measured by the probe is the
129	`handle_message` body alone.
130	
131	## Inner batching, warmup, clap (0.1.0-3)
132	
133	Adds CLI polish and measurement-quality improvements to Goal1.
134	Scope:
135	
136	- **Inner batching** — one probe scope now covers `--inner N`
137	  consecutive dispatches (default 1). At larger `N` the probe's
138	  two-`rdtsc`-plus-push apparatus cost is amortized, and stored
139	  tick values land in a range where the histogram's 0.1 %-relative
140	  buckets actually resolve jitter.
141	- **Warmup** — `--warmup SECS` (default **10.0**) runs the same
142	  dispatch loop with the probe active, then clears probe state;
143	  measurement phase inherits stable-state CPU freq / cache /
144	  branch-predictor conditions. Warmup message counts are never
145	  reported.
146	- **clap** — hand-rolled arg parsing replaced with a derive-based
147	  `Cli`. Validates non-negative finite `duration_s` and `warmup`;
148	  enforces `inner ≥ 1`. Auto-generated `--help` / `--version`.
149	- **TProbe2 additions** (vendored-code divergence from iiac-perf):
150	  - `Record` gains a `batch: u64` field.
151	  - `TProbe2::end_batch(id, n)` appends with `batch = n`; existing
152	    `end(id)` sets `batch = 1`.
153	  - `TProbe2::report` now divides stored deltas by `batch` at
154	    drain time so the histogram always holds per-event cost,

... [962 lines truncated] ...

message.content: Should they get license files, will make it easier to move in the future, but also noise for now

content.text:
Yes, I'd add them. The move-to-separate-repo property is the whole reason we did per-crate notes/READMEs; license files serve the same goal. Concrete benefits:

- `cargo package`/`cargo publish` only bundles files in the crate directory (not workspace-root ones) unless you rig `include =` in Cargo.toml. Per-crate license files mean the published crate on crates.io has them where users expect.
- Splitting into a separate repo becomes a clean `mv crates/tprobe ../tprobe-repo/` with nothing to chase down.

Cost is four static files that nobody reads and that basically never change. Small one-time noise, permanent benefit.

If approved I'll:

1. Copy `LICENSE-MIT` and `LICENSE-APACHE` from the workspace root to `crates/actor-x1/` and `crates/tprobe/`.
2. Update the READMEs so they stop pointing at the workspace-root license files (link to local ones instead).
3. Amend the 0.3.0-1 chore section + commit body.
4. Re-run the pre-commit checklist (fmt/clippy/test — unaffected by text files, but worth verifying).

Want me to roll this into 0.3.0-1 before we push?

message.content: y

# actor-x1

An experiment in the actor model in Rust (Communicating
Sequential Processes, Hoare 1978). Part of the
[actor-x1 workspace](../..); see the workspace
[README](../../README.md) for overview, usage, and
contribution conventions.

## Binaries

- **`goal1`** — two actors on one thread, ping-ponging an
  empty message through a shared `VecDeque`.
- **`goal2`** — two actors on two threads, ping-ponging
  over `std::sync::mpsc` channels.

Install from the workspace root:

```
cargo install --path crates/actor-x1
```

## Notes

- [`notes/design.md`](notes/design.md) — staged actor-model
  design. Stage 1 shipped at `0.1.0`; Stage 2 is next.

## License

Dual-licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0; license texts live at
the workspace root ([LICENSE-MIT](../../LICENSE-MIT),
[LICENSE-APACHE](../../LICENSE-APACHE)).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# tprobe

A named, single-writer performance probe that records
hardware tick-counter deltas across scoped code regions and
renders a percentile band-table report.

Part of the [actor-x1 workspace](../..). Originally vendored
from [`iiac-perf`](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf);
extracted into its own crate at `actor-x1 0.2.0`.

## At a glance

```rust
use tprobe::{TProbe, calibrate};

let mut probe = TProbe::new("dispatch");
let ovh = calibrate();

for _ in 0..n {
    let id = probe.start(site_id);
    // ... work to measure ...
    probe.end(id);
}

probe.report(/* as_ticks = */ false, Some(&ovh));
```

## Public surface

- `TProbe` / `TProbeRecId` — scope-based probe
  (`start` / `end` / `end_batch` → record buffer →
  histogram on `report`).
- `Overhead` / `calibrate` — apparatus-overhead calibration
  via a two-point fit.
- Modules `pin` (CPU affinity) and `ticks` (hardware tick
  counter).

## Notes

- [`notes/design.md`](notes/design.md) — architecture and
  hot-path / cold-path split.
- [`notes/overhead-model.md`](notes/overhead-model.md) —
  formal overhead model: what framing is, what
  `loop_per_iter` is, which overhead is unmeasurable and
  stays in the measurement, and the current subtraction
  policy.

## Platform

x86_64 only; `ticks.rs` emits `compile_error!` on other
architectures.

## License

Dual-licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0.

+  what ends up in the distributed artifact on crates.io.
+  Splitting a crate into its own repo later becomes a
+  straight directory move with nothing to chase down. Cost
+  is four static files that never change; the bot thinks

content.subject: 0.3.0: close ladder + README update
content.description: Final release commit (no -N suffix). Update top-level README.md to reflect: new output format with adj mean column, no --raw flag, 0.5s warmup default, new per-crate READMEs and notes layout. Move completed 0.3.0-* todos to Done.

content.subject: 0.3.0-3: band-table raw + adj mean column
content.description: TProbe::report stores raw per_event in histogram (no correction applied). band_table::render gains an optional per-event correction parameter; columns first/last/range/count/mean show raw; new rightmost adj mean column shows raw − correction. Summary rows: mean and mean min-p99 get adj values in the new column; stdev rows leave it blank (shift-invariant). Use global avg correction (approach a — exact for today's constant-batch PoC).
content.activeForm: Adding adj mean column to band table

content.subject: 0.3.0-2: extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter
content.description: Change Overhead::per_event_ticks to include both framing/batch and loop_per_iter_ticks. Update apparatus: line to show the split so readers see what was subtracted. Update tests. No flag/UX change yet.

content.subject: 0.3.0-1: notes reorg + per-crate README + overhead-model.md
content.description: Move notes/design.md → crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md. Create crates/actor-x1/notes/ and crates/tprobe/notes/. Add crates/actor-x1/README.md and crates/tprobe/README.md. Write crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md defining framing, loop-per-iter, and the "everything else" category (unmeasurable: cache misses, context switches, interrupts — stays in the number). Update code doc-comments in overhead.rs / tprobe.rs to point at overhead-model.md instead of carrying the long prose inline. Docs-only; no behavior change.

content.subject: 0.3.0-0: plan marker + version bump
content.description: Bump workspace version to 0.3.0-0. Add chores-01.md entry for the ladder. Update todo.md In Progress section.

content.subject: 0.3.0-4: remove --raw flag, always calibrate
content.description: Drop --raw CLI flag from goal1 and goal2. Calibration runs unconditionally (it's ~10ms). Remove the "raw (no overhead subtraction)" branch of the apparatus line. Update README.

content.subject: 0.3.0-5: lower warmup default to 0.5s
content.description: Change --warmup default from 10.0 to 0.5 in both goal1 and goal2. Update README.

content.text:
All checks clean. Diff now includes `LICENSE-MIT` and `LICENSE-APACHE` copied into each crate.

Updated proposed commit:

**Title** (unchanged)
```
docs: per-crate notes + overhead-model.md (0.3.0-1)
```

**Body** (adds license mention)
```
Docs-only step. Each crate gains its own `notes/` directory,
`README.md`, and `LICENSE-*` files so they travel with the
crate if the workspace ever splits into separate repos.
Formalizes the `tprobe` overhead vocabulary in a dedicated
`overhead-model.md` and shortens the long prose blocks in
`overhead.rs` / `lib.rs` to brief summaries plus pointers.

- `crates/actor-x1/Cargo.toml`: `0.3.0-0 → 0.3.0-1`.
- `crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md`: moved from
  `/notes/design.md` (unchanged content).
- `crates/actor-x1/README.md`: new per-crate README.
- `crates/actor-x1/LICENSE-MIT`, `crates/actor-x1/LICENSE-APACHE`:
  copied from workspace root.
- `crates/actor-x1/src/lib.rs`: `design.md` path updated.
- `crates/tprobe/README.md`: new per-crate README.
- `crates/tprobe/LICENSE-MIT`, `crates/tprobe/LICENSE-APACHE`:
  copied from workspace root.
- `crates/tprobe/notes/design.md`: new — architecture,
  hot/cold-path split, module layout, upstream history.
- `crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md`: new — formal
  definitions of framing and `loop_per_iter`, the
  unmeasurable category (cache misses, context switches,
  interrupts, …), current subtraction policy, and the
  per-band-correction extension if batches ever mix.
- `crates/tprobe/src/lib.rs`: 28-line header → 6 lines +
  notes pointers.
- `crates/tprobe/src/overhead.rs`: 42-line header → 10 lines
  + `notes/overhead-model.md` pointer.
- `README.md`, `notes/README.md`, `notes/chores-01.md`:
  `design.md` link targets retargeted to the new location
  (text preserved).
- `notes/chores-01.md`: new chore section for this step;
  linkme/inventory future-work section had its version
  suffix dropped since it isn't scheduled yet.
- `notes/todo.md`: `0.3.0-0` plan-marker → Done; `0.3.0-1`
  entry under In Progress; reference `[10]` added.
```

OK to run `vc-x1 push main`?

[package]
name = "actor-x1"
version = "0.3.0-1"
edition = "2024"
description = "An experiment in the actor model in Rust (Communicating Sequential Processes, Hoare 1978)."

input.old_string:
## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value. Currently:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch
                └── amortized over the scope
```

At `batch = 1` the full framing cost is paid per event; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events, rounding toward
zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is kept on the
`Overhead` struct for diagnostic visibility but is not
currently subtracted.

The bot thinks extending subtraction to include
`loop_per_iter_ticks` is the right call: loop scaffolding
is overhead relative to the dispatch work, and the
`black_box(1)` iter cost is a close-enough proxy. The
tradeoff is a small downward bias on reported values
because the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)`
itself; the bias is on the order of a tick per event and
worth the simpler story in the output. That change is
planned for a later step in the 0.3.0 ladder; this document
gets revised then.
input.new_string:
## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch + loop_per_iter_ticks
                └── amortized       └── paid per inner
                    over the scope      iteration
```

At `batch = 1` the framing term is paid in full; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events and rounds
toward zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is added
back because the real measurement loop pays per-iter
scaffolding (loop branch + counter increment) that the
empty-bench fit captures. The sum is rounded to the
nearest tick.

The bot thinks this matches "cost of the work alone" more
closely than framing-only subtraction: the real loop's
per-iter scaffolding is structural overhead relative to
the dispatch work, and `black_box(1)`'s iter cost is a
reasonable proxy. The tradeoff is a small downward bias —
the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)` itself,
so the measured `loop_per_iter_ticks` is slightly larger
than the real loop's scaffolding. The bot thinks the bias
is on the order of a tick per event and is worth the
simpler story in the output.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Overhead model

Definitions and policy for what `tprobe` calls "overhead"
and what gets subtracted from measured values.

## Measured quantities

Calibration runs a two-point fit on an empty `black_box(1)`
bench and produces two numbers, both recorded on the
`Overhead` struct.

### Framing — `framing_ticks` (per scope)

The hardware cost of the two `rdtsc` reads that bracket
every `TProbe` scope. Paid once per scope, independent of
how many events the scope covers. On modern x86 this is
~20 ticks (~5 ns at ~3.7 GHz).

Why two-point (and not single-point)? `rdtsc` is not a
serializing instruction, so two back-to-back reads with
nothing between them can execute out-of-order and measure
as little as 0–1 ticks — well below the true apparatus
cost. Bracketing with `_mm_lfence` drains the pipeline
harder than real work does and overcorrects. The two-point
fit avoids both biases by varying an `inner` size and
solving

```
raw_delta = framing + inner · loop_per_iter
```

in `f64` arithmetic (no integer truncation) across two
empty benches at `N_LOW = 100` and `N_HIGH = 10_000`. The
intercept is framing; the slope is `loop_per_iter` (below).

Noise amplification on the intercept is
`d(framing) / d(raw_low) = N_HIGH / (N_HIGH − N_LOW) ≈ 1.01`
with the current constants — any slop on `raw_low`
propagates roughly 1:1 to framing.

### Loop-per-iter — `loop_per_iter_ticks` (per inner iter)

The slope of the same fit — the per-iteration cost of the
calibration's empty inner loop, one `black_box(1)` per
iteration. On modern x86 this is small, on the order of one
tick per iter.

It models the loop scaffolding a real measurement loop also
pays: loop branch, counter increment, plus whatever cost a
"does-nothing" iteration has. It is not a perfect model of
a real dispatch loop — the real loop doesn't execute the
`black_box(1)` itself, so its per-iter scaffolding is
slightly cheaper than what calibration reports.

## Unmeasurable overhead

Things calibration does not separately measure and that are
not separately subtractable from stored scope deltas:

- Cache misses and branch mispredicts inside the scope.
- Context switches and OS interrupts during the scope.
- Microarchitectural noise (TLB misses, frequency changes,
  thermal throttling).

These are part of what the real system experiences and
remain in the measurement. The bot thinks they typically
manifest in the `p99-max` tail of the histogram — which is
why the band-table report also prints `mean min-p99`
(whole-distribution statistics excluding the top 1 % tail,
robust against rare outliers).

No future calibration can separate these per-event without
a fundamentally different measurement technique; they are
the irreducible noise floor of "whatever happened between
the two `rdtsc` reads". Keeping them in the measurement is
honest — they are part of the real cost the measured code
pays.

## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value. Currently:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch
                └── amortized over the scope
```

At `batch = 1` the full framing cost is paid per event; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events, rounding toward
zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is kept on the
`Overhead` struct for diagnostic visibility but is not
currently subtracted.

The bot thinks extending subtraction to include
`loop_per_iter_ticks` is the right call: loop scaffolding
is overhead relative to the dispatch work, and the
`black_box(1)` iter cost is a close-enough proxy. The
tradeoff is a small downward bias on reported values
because the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)`
itself; the bias is on the order of a tick per event and
worth the simpler story in the output. That change is
planned for a later step in the 0.3.0 ladder; this document
gets revised then.

## Per-event correction when batches mix

Today every record in a given probe drain carries the same
`batch` (the runtime commits to one `inner` per run), so
`correction` is a single constant across the drain. If a
future probe ever mixes `batch` values within one drain —
e.g. an actor whose scopes cover both fast-path singles
(`batch = 1`, framing term paid in full) and slow-path
batches (`batch = 1000`, framing term ≈ 0) — the correct
overhead-adjusted mean per band requires per-record
correction tracked per band, not a single global average.
The bot thinks the change at that point is:

- drain loop accumulates `band_correction_sum[i]` alongside
  `band_sum[i]`,
- `adj_mean[i] = (band_sum[i] - band_correction_sum[i]) /
  band_count[i]`.

Deferred until that situation arises.

- **License files copied into each crate.** Workspace root
  still has `LICENSE-MIT` / `LICENSE-APACHE`; each crate
  now also has its own copies. `cargo package` only bundles
  files inside the crate directory, so per-crate copies are
  what ends up in the distributed artifact on crates.io.
  Splitting a crate into its own repo later becomes a
  straight directory move with nothing to chase down. Cost
  is four static files that never change; the bot thinks
  the payoff outweighs the noise.

input.new_string:
- **License files copied into each crate.** Workspace root
  still has `LICENSE-MIT` / `LICENSE-APACHE`; each crate
  now also has its own copies. `cargo package` only bundles
  files inside the crate directory, so per-crate copies are
  what ends up in the distributed artifact on crates.io.
  Splitting a crate into its own repo later becomes a
  straight directory move with nothing to chase down. Cost
  is four static files that never change; the bot thinks
  the payoff outweighs the noise.

## Extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter (0.3.0-2)

Extends `Overhead::per_event_ticks` to include the
per-iteration `loop_per_iter_ticks` cost alongside the
per-scope amortized framing cost. The real measurement
loop pays per-iter scaffolding (loop branch + counter
increment) that the empty-bench calibration captures as
`loop_per_iter_ticks`; subtracting it matches "cost of the
work alone" more closely than framing-only subtraction.
The apparatus diagnostic line now shows both components so
readers can see the split. Behavior change is display-only
in the band-table body (raw stored values depend on the
correction); reported numbers drop by ~`loop_per_iter` per
event (typically on the order of one tick).

- `crates/actor-x1/Cargo.toml`: `0.3.0-1` → `0.3.0-2`.
- `crates/tprobe/src/overhead.rs`:
  `Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns
  `(framing_ticks / batch + loop_per_iter_ticks)` computed
  in `f64` and rounded to nearest `u64`. Docstring updated
  to describe both terms and point at `notes/overhead-model.md`.
  New test `per_event_ticks_adds_loop_per_iter` covers the
  combined formula at three batch sizes. Existing
  `per_event_ticks_amortizes_framing` still passes unchanged
  (it uses `loop_per_iter_ticks = 0.0`); comment tweaked
  from "truncates" to "rounds".
- `crates/actor-x1/src/bin/goal1.rs`,
  `crates/actor-x1/src/bin/goal2.rs`: `apparatus:` line
  gains a `loop_per_iter=X.XX tk (X.XX ns)` segment between
  the framing segment and the per-event total.
- `crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md`: subtraction-policy
  section rewritten to describe the combined formula. The
  earlier "planned for a later step" language is gone;
  rationale for including `loop_per_iter` stays (closer
  match to "cost of the work alone", small downward bias
  because the real loop doesn't run the `black_box(1)`).

### Design decisions recorded here

- **Round the combined correction, not each term
  independently.** Framing per event can be sub-tick at
  large `batch` (e.g. `21 / 1000 = 0.021`), and
  `loop_per_iter_ticks` can also be fractional. Adding in
  `f64` and rounding once preserves the combined precision
  (`0.021 + 1.2 ≈ 1.22 → 1`) rather than independently
  rounding each term down to zero and subtracting nothing.
- **`round().max(0.0) as u64`.** Calibration guarantees
  both terms are non-negative (framing clamps at 0,
  `loop_per_iter` does the same when the fit goes the
  wrong way), so the `max(0.0)` is belt-and-suspenders
  against an out-of-bounds cast. Cheap.
- **No change to histogram semantics.** `TProbe::report`
  still subtracts the per-event correction and clamps at
  1 (the histogram lower bound). Only the correction value
  changes; the subtraction path is untouched.

## Future work: linkme/inventory benchmark harness

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

- Notes reorg + per-crate READMEs + overhead-model.md (0.3.0-1) [[10]]

## Todo

See [Foramt details](README.md#todo-format)

- Initial design [[1]]
- PoC implementation of a manager
- Explore `linkme` / `inventory` for a benchmark harness on top of
  `tprobe` — self-registering benchmarks (no `main()` edits per
  benchmark); possibly reusable for actor registration too [[9]]
- Extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter (0.3.0-2)
- Band-table raw + `adj mean` column (0.3.0-3)
- Remove `--raw` flag, always calibrate (0.3.0-4)
- Lower warmup default 10 s → 0.5 s (0.3.0-5)
- After 0.3.0: add a `criterion` benchmark mirroring the goal1 /
  goal2 workloads, so `tprobe`'s numbers can be cross-validated
  against an established statistical-sampling harness

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- Stage 1 runtime PoC [[2]]
- Extract perf into tprobe crate — plan marker (0.2.0-0) [[3]]
- Workspace layout + tprobe skeleton (0.2.0-1) [[4]]
- Move perf into tprobe crate (0.2.0-2) [[5]]
- Rename TProbe2 → TProbe (0.2.0-3) [[6]]
- Extract perf into tprobe workspace crate (0.2.0) [[7]]
- Band-table format + overhead docs — plan marker (0.3.0-0) [[8]]


# References

[1]: ../crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md
[2]: chores-01.md#stage-1-complete-010
[3]: chores-01.md#extract-perf-into-tprobe-crate--plan-marker-020-0
[4]: chores-01.md#workspace-layout--tprobe-skeleton-020-1
[5]: chores-01.md#move-perf-into-tprobe-crate-020-2
[6]: chores-01.md#rename-tprobe2--tprobe-020-3
[7]: chores-01.md#workspace-split-complete-020
[8]: chores-01.md#band-table-format--overhead-docs--plan-marker-030-0
[9]: chores-01.md#future-work-linkmeinventory-benchmark-harness

content.subject: 0.3.0: close ladder + README update
content.description: Final release commit (no -N suffix). Update top-level README.md to reflect: new output format with adj mean column, no --raw flag, 0.5s warmup default, new per-crate READMEs and notes layout. Move completed 0.3.0-* todos to Done.

content.subject: 0.3.0-3: band-table raw + adj mean column
content.description: TProbe::report stores raw per_event in histogram (no correction applied). band_table::render gains an optional per-event correction parameter; columns first/last/range/count/mean show raw; new rightmost adj mean column shows raw − correction. Summary rows: mean and mean min-p99 get adj values in the new column; stdev rows leave it blank (shift-invariant). Use global avg correction (approach a — exact for today's constant-batch PoC).
content.activeForm: Adding adj mean column to band table

content.subject: 0.3.0-2: extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter
content.description: Change Overhead::per_event_ticks to include both framing/batch and loop_per_iter_ticks. Update apparatus: line to show the split so readers see what was subtracted. Update tests. No flag/UX change yet.

content.subject: 0.3.0-1: notes reorg + per-crate README + overhead-model.md
content.description: Move notes/design.md → crates/actor-x1/notes/design.md. Create crates/actor-x1/notes/ and crates/tprobe/notes/. Add crates/actor-x1/README.md and crates/tprobe/README.md. Write crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md defining framing, loop-per-iter, and the "everything else" category (unmeasurable: cache misses, context switches, interrupts — stays in the number). Update code doc-comments in overhead.rs / tprobe.rs to point at overhead-model.md instead of carrying the long prose inline. Docs-only; no behavior change.

content.subject: 0.3.0-0: plan marker + version bump
content.description: Bump workspace version to 0.3.0-0. Add chores-01.md entry for the ladder. Update todo.md In Progress section.

content.subject: 0.3.0-4: remove --raw flag, always calibrate
content.description: Drop --raw CLI flag from goal1 and goal2. Calibration runs unconditionally (it's ~10ms). Remove the "raw (no overhead subtraction)" branch of the apparatus line. Update README.

content.subject: 0.3.0-5: lower warmup default to 0.5s
content.description: Change --warmup default from 10.0 to 0.5 in both goal1 and goal2. Update README.

lastPrompt: The one thing I wonder if the clibration somewhere should use a outer + inner loop like we do? And then what does this do in runtime::run_for:             let Self {                 actors,…

content.text:
Both good questions. Let me lay them out side-by-side.

**Calibration already uses outer + inner — same shape as the runtime.**

`measure_empty_raw` in `overhead.rs`:

```rust
for _ in 0..samples {                // OUTER: many scopes
    let start = ticks::read_ticks(); // rdtsc_start (like probe.start)
    for _ in 0..inner {              // INNER: N units per scope
        black_box(1u64);             // "work"
    }
    let end = ticks::read_ticks();   // rdtsc_end (like probe.end)
    min_ticks = min_ticks.min(end - start);
}
```

`SingleThreadRuntime::run_for` in `runtime.rs`:

```rust
while Instant::now() < deadline {           // OUTER: scopes until time's up
    let id = probe.start(first_dst as u64); // rdtsc_start
    while done < inner {                    // INNER: up to `inner` dispatches
        // pop queue, dispatch handle_message
    }
    probe.end_batch(id, inner);              // rdtsc_end
}
```

Same shape. The per-scope structure — rdtsc, inner loop, rdtsc — matches exactly. Two deliberate differences in what's around it:

- **Inner body**: calibration runs `black_box(1u64)` (apparatus only); runtime runs `handle_message` (work). That's the whole point — calibration measures what the apparatus adds on top of an almost-empty inner.
- **Outer reduction**: calibration takes the `min` across outer iterations to pin down the floor; runtime records every scope because it wants the full distribution. The `samples` loop is calibration's analogue of "run for some time", just sized for floor estimation (1000 samples is enough for convergence, keeps total wall-clock <10 ms).

So the answer is: we're already doing what you're suggesting. If the inner-loop body diverged structurally from the runtime's — say, calibration missed a loop counter increment that the runtime pays — the fit would be biased. But they match (tight for-loop with a single no-op inside), which is why `loop_per_iter_ticks` is a reasonable proxy for the runtime's per-iter scaffolding.

If you'd like I can add a note in `overhead-model.md` spelling out this structural correspondence; it's currently implicit.

**The `let Self { … } = self;` destructuring in `run_for`**

It's Rust's idiom for **split-borrow** (a.k.a. disjoint-borrow). It lets the borrow checker see three independent `&mut` bindings (`actors`, `queue`, `probe`) instead of a single `&mut self`.

The inner loop needs to do three things simultaneously:
1. `&mut actors[dst]` — pick the actor.
2. `&mut queue` — pass into `SingleCtx` so `handle_message` can enqueue new messages.
3. `probe.start/end_batch` — straddle the batch with rdtsc calls.

With plain `self.actors[dst]` + `self.queue` + `self.probe`, borrow-chk can't always prove those paths are disjoint when they're used together across the inner loop (the compiler's partial-borrow inference is per-expression, not across nested scopes and closures). The destructure flattens `self` into three independent bindings with no common aliasing; each can be borrowed mutably at will.

Concretely, without it this line would fail:

```rust
let actor = &mut self.actors[dst as usize];
let mut ctx = SingleCtx { queue: &mut self.queue };  // second &mut self — error
```

With the destructure, `actors` and `queue` are already separate roots:

```rust
let actor = &mut actors[dst as usize];
let mut ctx = SingleCtx { queue };  // reborrow of the `queue` binding
```

No `self` involved after the destructure, so nothing for the borrow checker to reject.

The runtime.rs struct docstring at line 64 (`"field-split-borrows actors and queue"`) already names the pattern, but doesn't explain it. I can add an inline comment on the `let Self { … } = self;` line noting the reason, if that would help readers.

Good point — `self.probe.start(first_dst as u64)` on its own works fine (it just borrows `self.probe` for the duration of the call and releases). The destructure isn't there for `probe.start`; it's to satisfy the inner loop's simultaneous `&mut actors[i]` + `&mut queue` borrows.

But you're making me look again — Rust has supported disjoint `&mut` through named field paths for a long time, so the inner loop might compile without the destructure too. Let me test.

content.text:
Confirmed: clippy clean, 23/23 tests pass. The destructure was indeed dead code — Rust sees `&mut self.actors[dst]` and `&mut self.queue` as disjoint field borrows and lets them coexist. So the three-line pattern came from a phase where I either hit a transient borrow-check issue or wrote it defensively without checking.

A couple of housekeeping bits if we keep the simplification:

1. The `SingleThreadRuntime` doc comment (runtime.rs:64) says "field-split-borrows `actors` and `queue`" — that's describing the now-removed pattern, needs updating.

2. Scope question: this cleanup is orthogonal to 0.3.0-2's stated scope (overhead subtraction). CLAUDE.md's "don't bundle unrelated refactors" leans toward splitting. But the change is trivial (one block + one doc line) and you prompted it.

Three options:

(a) **Fold into 0.3.0-2.** Amend the commit body to mention the simplification; update the doc comment. One commit, clean.

(b) **Back it out of 0.3.0-2, push -2 as planned, do the cleanup as its own step later.** Would need a `0.3.0-2b` or similar (not a pattern we've used) or a separate follow-up at 0.3.1. More churn for a tiny change.

(c) **Back it out entirely.** You've seen the answer; don't actually change the code. Cheapest in terms of session churn.

I lean (a). Your call.

All clean (fmt / clippy / 23 tests). `cargo install` + apparatus-line retest from before is still valid (only code revert was the runtime.rs restoration).

Proposed commit:

**Title**
```
feat: subtract framing + loop_per_iter (0.3.0-2)
```

**Body**
```
Extends `Overhead::per_event_ticks` to include
`loop_per_iter_ticks` alongside the amortized framing cost.
The real measurement loop pays per-iter scaffolding (loop
branch + counter increment) that calibration already
captures as `loop_per_iter_ticks`; subtracting it matches
"cost of the work alone" more closely than framing-only.
The apparatus diagnostic line now shows both components.
Reported per-event values drop by ~loop_per_iter per event
(typically ~1 tick).

- `crates/actor-x1/Cargo.toml`: `0.3.0-1 → 0.3.0-2`.
- `crates/tprobe/src/overhead.rs`: `per_event_ticks(batch)`
  returns `framing_ticks / batch + loop_per_iter_ticks`
  computed in `f64` and rounded to nearest `u64` with a
  non-negative clamp. Docstring + pointer at
  `notes/overhead-model.md`. New test
  `per_event_ticks_adds_loop_per_iter` covers the combined
  formula at three batch sizes; existing
  `per_event_ticks_amortizes_framing` still passes.
- `crates/actor-x1/src/bin/goal1.rs`,
  `crates/actor-x1/src/bin/goal2.rs`: `apparatus:` line
  gains a `loop_per_iter=X.XX tk (X.XX ns)` segment between
  framing and per-event total.
- `crates/tprobe/notes/overhead-model.md`: subtraction-policy
  section rewritten to describe the combined formula; earlier
  "planned for a later step" language removed.
- `notes/chores-01.md`: new `## Extend subtraction to
  framing + loop_per_iter (0.3.0-2)` section with three
  design decisions (round the sum not the terms;
  `max(0.0)` belt-and-suspenders; no histogram semantics
  change).
- `notes/todo.md`: `0.3.0-1` → Done; `0.3.0-2` → In Progress;
  reference `[11]` added. New follow-up item: remove the
  unnecessary `Self` destructure in
  `SingleThreadRuntime::run_for` — disjoint `&mut` through
  named field paths compiles without the split.
```

OK to run `vc-x1 push main`?

message.content: Another obvservation, why use a while in one and a for in the other, I suspect the generated code is the same but I'd lean towards a for loop in the inner timing loops

content.text:
Good eye. Same observation, different loop styles:

- Calibration inner: `for _ in 0..inner { black_box(1u64); }` — idiomatic, known bound.
- Runtime inner: `while done < inner { …; done += 1; }` — manual counter plus `break` on queue empty.

**Generated code**: under `--release` with LLVM's loop optimizations, these compile to the same thing (same CFG, same induction variable). No measurable difference.

**Idiom**: I agree, `for` reads better when the upper bound is known. The runtime's `while` form only exists because we also need to `break` on `queue.pop_front() → None` (partial batch); but `for _ in 0..inner { let Some(...) = queue.pop_front() else { break }; …; done += 1; }` does exactly that and is cleaner. `done` stays a separate counter either way (to detect partial batches after the loop).

**Where it fits**: this cleanup is the same inner-loop block as the `Self` destructure we already have a todo for. I'd roll them into one cleanup item so both get done in the same micro-step.

Shall I update that todo entry to cover both, or add a separate one?

message.content: one entry for both, continue

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

- Extend subtraction to framing + loop_per_iter (0.3.0-2) [[11]]

## Todo

See [Foramt details](README.md#todo-format)

- Initial design [[1]]
- PoC implementation of a manager
- Explore `linkme` / `inventory` for a benchmark harness on top of
  `tprobe` — self-registering benchmarks (no `main()` edits per
  benchmark); possibly reusable for actor registration too [[9]]
- Band-table raw + `adj mean` column (0.3.0-3)
- Remove `--raw` flag, always calibrate (0.3.0-4)
- Lower warmup default 10 s → 0.5 s (0.3.0-5)
- After 0.3.0: add a `criterion` benchmark mirroring the goal1 /
  goal2 workloads, so `tprobe`'s numbers can be cross-validated
  against an established statistical-sampling harness
- Remove unnecessary `let Self { actors, queue, probe } = self;`
  destructure in `SingleThreadRuntime::run_for`. Disjoint `&mut`
  through named field paths (`self.actors[i]`, `self.queue`,
  `self.probe`) compiles without the split. Also update the
  struct doc comment that references "field-split-borrows
  actors and queue"

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- Band-table format + overhead docs — plan marker (0.3.0-0) [[8]]
- Notes reorg + per-crate READMEs + overhead-model.md (0.3.0-1) [[10]]

input.old_string:
## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch + loop_per_iter_ticks
                └── amortized       └── paid per inner
                    over the scope      iteration
```

At `batch = 1` the framing term is paid in full; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events and rounds
toward zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is added
back because the real measurement loop pays per-iter
scaffolding (loop branch + counter increment) that the
empty-bench fit captures. The sum is rounded to the
nearest tick.

The bot thinks this matches "cost of the work alone" more
closely than framing-only subtraction: the real loop's
per-iter scaffolding is structural overhead relative to
the dispatch work, and `black_box(1)`'s iter cost is a
reasonable proxy. The tradeoff is a small downward bias —
the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)` itself,
so the measured `loop_per_iter_ticks` is slightly larger
than the real loop's scaffolding. The bot thinks the bias
is on the order of a tick per event and is worth the
simpler story in the output.
input.new_string:
## Correction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch + loop_per_iter_ticks
                └── amortized       └── paid per inner
                    over the scope      iteration
```

At `batch = 1` the framing term is paid in full; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events and rounds
toward zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is added
because the real measurement loop pays per-iter scaffolding
(loop branch + counter increment) that the empty-bench fit
captures. The sum is rounded to the nearest tick.

The bot thinks this matches "cost of the work alone" more
closely than framing-only: the real loop's per-iter
scaffolding is structural overhead relative to the dispatch
work, and `black_box(1)`'s iter cost is a reasonable proxy.
The tradeoff is a small downward bias — the real loop
doesn't execute the `black_box(1)` itself, so the measured
`loop_per_iter_ticks` is slightly larger than the real
loop's scaffolding. The bot thinks the bias is on the order
of a tick per event and is worth the simpler story in the
output.

### Where the correction is applied

`TProbe`'s histogram stores **raw** per-event values
unchanged — no in-place subtraction. The correction is
averaged across drained records and passed to the
band-table renderer, which displays it in a rightmost
`adj mean` column alongside the raw columns. Each row's
(and each mean summary row's) raw mean has the correction
subtracted, clamped at 0, for display; the other columns
(`first` / `last` / `range` / `count` / `mean`) show raw
unchanged.

`stdev` rows leave the `adj mean` column blank: standard
deviation is shift-invariant, so subtracting a constant
from every sample doesn't change it.

The bot thinks raw-and-adjusted side-by-side is more
honest than hiding raw behind a subtracted mean: readers
see the physical per-event cost the probe actually
measured plus a derived "cost of the work alone" column,
and band placement + distribution shape are unaffected by
the correction choice.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Overhead model

Definitions and policy for what `tprobe` calls "overhead"
and what gets subtracted from measured values.

## Measured quantities

Calibration runs a two-point fit on an empty `black_box(1)`
bench and produces two numbers, both recorded on the
`Overhead` struct.

### Framing — `framing_ticks` (per scope)

The hardware cost of the two `rdtsc` reads that bracket
every `TProbe` scope. Paid once per scope, independent of
how many events the scope covers. On modern x86 this is
~20 ticks (~5 ns at ~3.7 GHz).

Why two-point (and not single-point)? `rdtsc` is not a
serializing instruction, so two back-to-back reads with
nothing between them can execute out-of-order and measure
as little as 0–1 ticks — well below the true apparatus
cost. Bracketing with `_mm_lfence` drains the pipeline
harder than real work does and overcorrects. The two-point
fit avoids both biases by varying an `inner` size and
solving

```
raw_delta = framing + inner · loop_per_iter
```

in `f64` arithmetic (no integer truncation) across two
empty benches at `N_LOW = 100` and `N_HIGH = 10_000`. The
intercept is framing; the slope is `loop_per_iter` (below).

Noise amplification on the intercept is
`d(framing) / d(raw_low) = N_HIGH / (N_HIGH − N_LOW) ≈ 1.01`
with the current constants — any slop on `raw_low`
propagates roughly 1:1 to framing.

### Loop-per-iter — `loop_per_iter_ticks` (per inner iter)

The slope of the same fit — the per-iteration cost of the
calibration's empty inner loop, one `black_box(1)` per
iteration. On modern x86 this is small, on the order of one
tick per iter.

It models the loop scaffolding a real measurement loop also
pays: loop branch, counter increment, plus whatever cost a
"does-nothing" iteration has. It is not a perfect model of
a real dispatch loop — the real loop doesn't execute the
`black_box(1)` itself, so its per-iter scaffolding is
slightly cheaper than what calibration reports.

## Unmeasurable overhead

Things calibration does not separately measure and that are
not separately subtractable from stored scope deltas:

- Cache misses and branch mispredicts inside the scope.
- Context switches and OS interrupts during the scope.
- Microarchitectural noise (TLB misses, frequency changes,
  thermal throttling).

These are part of what the real system experiences and
remain in the measurement. The bot thinks they typically
manifest in the `p99-max` tail of the histogram — which is
why the band-table report also prints `mean min-p99`
(whole-distribution statistics excluding the top 1 % tail,
robust against rare outliers).

No future calibration can separate these per-event without
a fundamentally different measurement technique; they are
the irreducible noise floor of "whatever happened between
the two `rdtsc` reads". Keeping them in the measurement is
honest — they are part of the real cost the measured code
pays.

## Subtraction policy

`Overhead::per_event_ticks(batch)` returns the per-event
correction `TProbe::report` subtracts from every stored
value:

```
correction = framing_ticks / batch + loop_per_iter_ticks
                └── amortized       └── paid per inner
                    over the scope      iteration
```

At `batch = 1` the framing term is paid in full; at
`batch = N` it is spread across `N` events and rounds
toward zero as `N` grows. `loop_per_iter_ticks` is added
back because the real measurement loop pays per-iter
scaffolding (loop branch + counter increment) that the
empty-bench fit captures. The sum is rounded to the
nearest tick.

The bot thinks this matches "cost of the work alone" more
closely than framing-only subtraction: the real loop's
per-iter scaffolding is structural overhead relative to
the dispatch work, and `black_box(1)`'s iter cost is a
reasonable proxy. The tradeoff is a small downward bias —
the real loop doesn't execute the `black_box(1)` itself,
so the measured `loop_per_iter_ticks` is slightly larger
than the real loop's scaffolding. The bot thinks the bias
is on the order of a tick per event and is worth the
simpler story in the output.

## Per-event correction when batches mix

Today every record in a given probe drain carries the same
`batch` (the runtime commits to one `inner` per run), so
`correction` is a single constant across the drain. If a
future probe ever mixes `batch` values within one drain —
e.g. an actor whose scopes cover both fast-path singles
(`batch = 1`, framing term paid in full) and slow-path
batches (`batch = 1000`, framing term ≈ 0) — the correct
overhead-adjusted mean per band requires per-record
correction tracked per band, not a single global average.
The bot thinks the change at that point is:

- drain loop accumulates `band_correction_sum[i]` alongside
  `band_sum[i]`,
- `adj_mean[i] = (band_sum[i] - band_correction_sum[i]) /
  band_count[i]`.

Deferred until that situation arises.
